facts.fact: There are more stars in the observable universe than grains of sand on every beach and desert on Earth — roughly 10 times more.
A day on Venus is longer than a year on Venus. It takes 243 Earth days to rotate once, but only 225 Earth days to orbit the Sun.
Neutron stars are so dense that a teaspoon of their material would weigh about 10 million tons on Earth.
The Voyager 1 spacecraft, launched in 1977, is now over 24 billion kilometers from Earth — and it's still sending us data.
Saturn's rings are only about 10 meters thick on average, despite being 282,000 kilometers wide. Proportionally thinner than a sheet of paper.
There is a giant cloud of alcohol floating in space — Sagittarius B2 contains enough ethyl alcohol to fill 400 trillion trillion pints of beer.
The Sun loses about 4 million tons of mass every single second through nuclear fusion — and has been doing so for 4.6 billion years.
If you removed all the empty space from atoms in every human on Earth, all 8 billion of us would fit in a cube the size of a sugar cube.
Your body produces about 25 million new cells every second. In the time it takes to read this sentence, you've made more cells than there are people in Australia.
The human brain can store approximately 2.5 petabytes of information — equivalent to 3 million hours of TV shows.
Octopuses have three hearts, nine brains, and blue blood. Two hearts pump blood to the gills, one to the rest of the body, and each arm has its own mini-brain.
The tardigrade can survive in the vacuum of space, withstand radiation 1000x the lethal dose for humans, and live for 30 years without food or water.
Cleopatra lived closer in time to the Moon landing than to the construction of the Great Pyramid of Giza.
Your stomach acid is strong enough to dissolve razor blades — it has a pH of 1.5 to 3.5, similar to battery acid.
Humans share 60% of their DNA with bananas, 85% with mice, and 98.7% with chimpanzees.
The electric eel can generate up to 860 volts of electricity — enough to power 10 lightbulbs or knock a horse unconscious.
A single strand of human DNA, fully uncoiled, is about 2 meters long. If you uncoiled all the DNA in your body it would stretch to Pluto and back — 17 times.
Mantis shrimp can throw a punch at 80 km/h, generating a force of 1,500 Newtons — enough to shatter aquarium glass.
If you fell into a black hole, an outside observer would see you freeze and slowly fade away. You, however, would experience nothing unusual as you crossed the event horizon.
Light takes 8 minutes to travel from the Sun to Earth. The sunlight you're seeing right now left the Sun before you had your last cup of tea.
Time literally moves faster at higher altitudes due to gravity's effect on spacetime. GPS satellites have to correct for this or they'd be off by about 10 km per day.
Quantum entanglement allows two particles to instantly affect each other regardless of distance — Einstein called it 'spooky action at a distance' and it's been experimentally verified.
At absolute zero (-273.15°C), atoms nearly stop moving entirely. Scientists have cooled matter to within a billionth of a degree above it.
The Planck length (1.6 × 10⁻³⁵ meters) is so small that if an atom were scaled to the size of the observable universe, the Planck length would be the size of an actual atom.
The Oxford University is older than the Aztec Empire. Teaching started at Oxford around 1096 AD; the Aztec Empire was founded in 1428 AD.
Nintendo was founded in 1889 — 32 years before the first commercial radio broadcast. It started as a playing card company.
The fax machine was invented in 1843 — 33 years before the telephone. You could fax documents before you could call anyone.
Woolly mammoths were still alive when the Great Pyramid of Giza was being built. A small population survived on Wrangel Island until 1650 BC.
The shortest war in recorded history lasted 38 minutes — Britain vs Zanzibar in 1896. Zanzibar surrendered before the British had finished breakfast.
There are more possible iterations of a game of chess than there are atoms in the observable universe — roughly 10^120 possible games.
The Great Wall of China is not visible from space with the naked eye — that's a myth. But it is longer than the distance from Earth to the Moon.
Ancient Romans used crushed mouse brains as toothpaste. They were also known to use urine as mouthwash — and it actually worked due to the ammonia content.
The first computer bug was a literal bug — a moth found trapped in a relay of the Harvard Mark II computer in 1947. Grace Hopper's team taped it into the logbook.
The Apollo 11 guidance computer that took humans to the Moon had 4KB of RAM and ran at 0.043 MHz. Your phone is roughly 100,000,000 times more powerful.
The total amount of data generated globally every day is approximately 2.5 quintillion bytes. 90% of all data ever created was generated in the last two years.
Email is older than the World Wide Web. Ray Tomlinson sent the first email in 1971 — the web wasn't invented until 1989.
There are more transistors on a modern GPU than there are neurons in a human brain. Nvidia's H100 has 80 billion transistors; your brain has ~86 billion neurons.
The first 1GB hard drive, released by IBM in 1980, weighed 250 kg and cost $40,000. Today a 1GB microSD card costs less than a cup of coffee.
If you shuffle a deck of cards properly, the exact order you get has almost certainly never existed before in history — and never will again. There are 8×10^67 possible arrangements.

The number googol (10^100) is smaller than the number of possible games of chess (10^120), which is itself dwarfed by Graham's number — a number so large it can't be written in the observable universe.
In a room of just 23 people, there is a greater than 50% chance that two of them share the same birthday. With 70 people, the probability reaches 99.9%.
Pi has been calculated to over 100 trillion decimal places and no pattern has ever been found. It is, as far as we know, infinite and non-repeating.
Lightning strikes Earth approximately 100 times every single second — that's 8 million times per day and about 3 billion times per year.
The Earth's core is approximately the same temperature as the surface of the Sun — around 5,500°C.
There is more water locked in Earth's mantle than in all the oceans on the surface combined — stored inside a mineral called ringwoodite.
The Pacific Ocean is so large that all of Earth's continents could fit inside it with room to spare.
Mount Everest is not the tallest mountain on Earth — Mauna Kea in Hawaii is, when measured from its oceanic base. It stands 10,210 meters tall vs Everest's 8,849 meters.
The human brain cannot multitask — it rapidly switches between tasks, each switch costing time and cognitive energy. True parallel processing in humans is a myth.
Your brain is more active during deep sleep than during many waking hours — it uses sleep to flush out toxic waste products that build up during the day.
The 'doorway effect' is a real phenomenon — walking through a doorway causes your brain to file away the previous room's context, which is why you forget why you walked in.
You cannot hum while holding your nose closed. Try it.
Sharks are older than trees. Sharks have existed for ~450 million years; trees only evolved ~350 million years ago. Sharks survived all five mass extinctions.
The largest known star, Stephenson 2-18, is so massive that if placed at the center of our solar system, its surface would extend past Saturn's orbit.
A proton travels around the Large Hadron Collider at CERN 11,245 times per second — that's 99.9999991% the speed of light.
Humans have only explored about 20% of Earth's oceans. We have better maps of the Moon and Mars than we do of our own ocean floor.
